region_of_origin: Bengal, India (popularized in the United Kingdom)
food_subgroup: Cooked chicken dishes — dry curry / stir-fry

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Chicken Jalfrezi; mapped to the broader 'chicken dish' class as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a cooked chicken preparation with vegetables.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Chicken Jalfrezi; A00GJ (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible match. Vegetable and spice components are not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Chicken Jalfrezi or a sufficiently close curry preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of Chicken Jalfrezi is approximately 250 g, typically plated as a main dish alongside rice or flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, stewed (NDB 05013) combined with vegetable component estimates from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / ICMR-National Institute of Nutrition, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Chicken Jalfrezi is not listed as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from USDA cooked chicken entries and ICMR-NIN data for Indian mixed chicken dishes with vegetables. The composite reflects approximately 55% chicken, 30% mixed vegetables (bell peppers, onions, tomatoes), and 15% oil and spice base. Input AI-generated values were cross-checked against these sources and retained where consistent.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate based on mixed chicken and vegetable dish with low starch content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care, 2008.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Jalfrezi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) the dish being predominantly protein and fat with low net carbohydrate (5.4 g/100g); (2) high dietary fibre from bell peppers and onions attenuating glucose response; (3) comparable low-GI mixed meat-and-vegetable dishes in the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 13.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.7 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 320 mg per 100 g (800 mg per standard 250 g serving) is a meaningful contributor to daily sodium intake for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation, limit portion size to 150–200 g, avoid pairing with high-sodium condiments, and ensure total daily sodium remains below 1500–2000 mg as recommended by clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI 35) and high lean protein content support postprandial glucose stability; net carbohydrate per serving is approximately 13.5 g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Jalfrezi is a suitable main dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled and paired with a low-GI carbohydrate such as basmati rice or whole-wheat roti, and when oil quantity is controlled to limit total caloric load per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (approximately 2.4 g/100 g) and sodium (320 mg/100 g) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; bell peppers and tomatoes contribute beneficial antioxidants including vitamin C and lycopene.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Chicken Jalfrezi should be prepared with minimal oil (preferably unsaturated oil such as canola or olive oil), skinless chicken breast, and reduced salt, while the antioxidant-rich vegetable content supports endothelial health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Turmeric and ginger in the spice base provide curcumin and gingerol with documented anti-inflammatory properties; however, nightshade vegetables (bell peppers, tomatoes) may be triggers in a subset of RA patients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Most individuals with rheumatoid arthritis can consume Chicken Jalfrezi without concern, but those who identify nightshade sensitivity should trial elimination of bell peppers and tomatoes and monitor symptom response.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Jalfrezi is a spicy, vibrant stir-fry dish originating in the Bengal region of the Indian subcontinent during the British Raj era, and subsequently popularized across the United Kingdom where it became one of the most ordered curry-house dishes. The name derives from the Bengali word 'jhāl' (spicy) and 'frezi' (stir-fry). The dish is prepared by marinating chicken pieces — typically boneless breast or thigh — in a blend of turmeric, cumin, coriander, and chili, then stir-frying them at high heat with sliced bell peppers, onions, tomatoes, fresh green chilies, ginger, and garlic. A distinguishing characteristic is its relatively dry consistency compared to saucy curries, achieved by cooking off excess liquid. A dash of vinegar or lime juice at the end imparts a tangy-spicy finish. Per 100 g, Chicken Jalfrezi provides approximately 152 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 9.2 g fat, and 5.4 g carbohydrate, making it a protein-dense, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate main dish. It is rich in vitamin C (45 mg/100 g) from bell peppers, and contains meaningful amounts of niacin, vitamin B6, and selenium from the chicken component. The estimated glycemic index is low (GI ~35), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Clinically, the dish is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled and paired with low-GI carbohydrates. Sodium content (320 mg/100 g) warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. The anti-inflammatory spice base (turmeric, ginger) adds functional food value. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal-compliant when prepared with halal-certified chicken.

confidence_reason: Chicken Jalfrezi is not listed as a single composite entry in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA cooked chicken data and ICMR-NIN Indian mixed dish data, cross-validated against the AI-generated input values which were internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate with no direct measurement available. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the composite Chicken Jalfrezi preparation is not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN for this specific dish; estimated water content would require full recipe moisture analysis. No approved source consulted (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN) provides a direct water_g value for this composite preparation. Saturated fat (2.4 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.8 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.1 g), trans_fat_g (0.0 g), and cholesterol_mg (55 mg) values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken and vegetable oil component data and are not directly measured for this composite dish. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for Chicken Jalfrezi was found. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (35 × 13.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.7 g (low GL). This dish is not a Singaporean dish; G6 multilingual compliance does not require Chinese, Malay, or Tamil names. Hindi is provided as the dish originates from the Indian subcontinent. Bengali and Urdu aliases are preserved from the input. No SNOMED CT code was found for this dish or a sufficiently close curry preparation; snomed_ct_match_type set to 'none' with gap note. FoodEx2 match type corrected to 'closest_parent' (from 'adapted') to conform to allowed values.
